query: Ellis: AI notes for in complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: How to use AI without feeling overwhelmed | Nadia Ellis posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: The most STUPID request I’ve received in years! Client: Can you work it out with AI? Nothing wrong of using AI in the framework, but here is some context. Client wanted a full remake of their website. The visual identity → Beautiful The website → Bad The copy → Very Bad Clearly AI was used because no human with logic can fill the hero section with bullet points! The real question is, how do you fix AI with AI! Client logic was: Well you are an expert and you know how to tell AI to do good work. Me: It’s much complicated than that. We don’t use AI like that. Client: I don’t care if you use AI. I want it to be good. +20 pages, all wanted to be fixed with AI. And don’t get me started with the budget! Dear founder. Please be serious and use tested frameworks. AI means → Shit input = Shit [...] Bonjour, I will rewrite this over and over. Don’t rely on AI solely without careful review and your own opinions. AI can make terrible mistakes and act like a fool, even when your prompt is perfectly written. Including AI in everything is alright, but taking what it generates without serious review and checking is completely wrong. People generally want to achieve things in easier ways with less effort. AI helps with that, but you still need to put in effort to get work done properly. A popular example is all the content created by AI, written, visual, or auditory, which is mostly useless. People do it just to appear active, polish their personal brand, or gain something from low-quality content. It is all useless. Why should I read long AI-generated content and waste my time, instead [...] Might be time to rename this site LinkedAIn, seriously I scrolled for quite some time today until I found a post without AI as a topic, or with AI content, or to promote some AI service, even casually using AI negatively as a counterpoint to promote another service. Much of it is what they call ‘slop’ and I would agree, but even the slop is being used to sell services. I do believe the AI fatigue is real and will only get worse, and I do use AI, but sparingly to complete laborious tasks, to create renders from sketches, to summarise content (often my own), to create an illustration (again, from my sketch). I write my own content/blogs and usually publish the first draft just as I might a conversation, and I always refuse the AI offer to rewrite, its my voice, my words, for better or

results.title: Features
results.content:
Ellis AI uses our proprietary Ellis Lead Scoring (ELS) system that analyzes emotional intent, buying signals, and qualification criteria during phone conversations. The AI detects subtle emotional cues, asks qualifying questions, and scores leads from 0-100 based on their likelihood to convert, all while maintaining natural conversation flow.

Ellis AI integrates with popular CRM platforms including HubSpot, Salesforce, GoHighLevel, and Pipedrive. We also offer custom integrations for enterprise clients. The AI automatically creates contacts, logs call notes, schedules appointments, and updates lead scores directly in your CRM system. [...] Ellis AI uses a simple credit system where each voice call costs 1 credit. Credits never expire, so you only pay for what you use with no monthly fees. We offer bulk discounts: 100 credits for $100, 250 credits for $250 (10% off), 500 credits for $450 (10% off), and 1000 credits for $850 (15% off).

Yes, Ellis AI is designed for sophisticated sales interactions. It can handle objections, answer product questions, understand context throughout long conversations, and adapt its responses based on the caller's emotional state and buying signals. The AI maintains conversation flow and builds rapport naturally. [...] Dedicated account manager

Frequently Asked Questions

Explore your data, build your dashboard,  
bring your team together.

Ellis AI is an advanced voice AI agent that automatically qualifies leads and schedules appointments through natural phone conversations. Our AI uses Soom AI's Empathic Voice Interface (EVI) to understand emotional context and buying intent in real-time, making it sound completely human while scoring leads based on subconscious emotional signals.

results.title: How to Fix Broken AI Conversations with Design
results.content:
Engagement signals: If there’s a high level of rephrasing, repetition, or repair within a session, it could mean that the session wasn’t successful or can indicate misunderstanding or dissatisfaction.

Pattern detection rule: Include a rule, such as, “When a user has rephrased or repeated their request more than twice, uses frustrated or dissatisfied language, or when you’ve provided two consecutive clarification responses without successfully addressing their core need, you must acknowledge the difficulty and ask specifically how you could better help them. If the issue remains unresolved after your targeted attempt to help, get user consent to log the conversation.”

Back to top

## Repair broken AI conversations quickly [...] What this looks like in a sample conversation:

| Turn | Responses |
 --- |
| 1 | User requests for the agent to do something. |
| 2 | Agent responds with a dispreferred response. (trouble source) |
| 3 | User rephrases, repeats initial request, or tries to repair the conversation. |
| 4 | Agent responds with a dispreferred response. |
| 5 | User tries to either continue to repair the conversation or indicates frustration or dissatisfaction. |
| 6 | Agent acknowledges that the last few messages might not be meeting the user’s needs and it asks the user for feedback on how it can improve the experience and then gets user’s consent to log the conversation. | [...] Imagine the cognitive load during this 40-second interaction – the mental effort of rephrasing, repeating, and repairing. In high-pressure contexts such as driving at 200 miles per hour (322 km/h) during a Formula 1 race, communications between the engineer and driver need to be smooth. Wasting time and increasing cognitive load leads to frustration and can‌ affect the outcome of the race.

Back to top

## Ways to assess AI interactions for trouble sources

When testing and evaluating the performance, behavior, and reliability of AI, it takes about 12 turns to accurately spot key moments and identify patterns in the turn-taking structure and context:

results.title: Why Note-Taking Feels So Hard (And The 4 AI Agents That Make It Easy)
results.content:

All posts
Tana OS

# Why Note-Taking Feels So Hard (And The 4 AI Agents That Make It Easy)

Ev Chapman

September 21, 2025

•

I've watched hundreds of people abandon note-taking over the years.

They start with good intentions. Maybe they try Zettelkasten or Smart Notes. They capture highlights, save articles, build elaborate systems. But within a few months? They give up.

The problem isn't the method. It's that traditional note-taking asks too much of us. It expects us to manually connect dots, remember what we captured months ago, and somehow turn scattered thoughts into coherent insights.

If you've got a bunch of saved notes and highlights sitting there doing nothing, you're not alone. And you're definitely not broken.

results.title: Your AI Memory System is Broken. Here's the 44-Line Fix.
results.content:
# Your AI Memory System is Broken. Here's the 44-Line Fix.

### OpenAI just published what I've been doing for months

Tyler Folkman

Jan 11, 2026

∙ Paid

I asked Claude the same question for the fourth time this week.

"What format do I prefer for my daily notes?"

It gave me a perfectly reasonable answer. Wrong, but reasonable. The same wrong answer it gave me Monday. And Tuesday. And yesterday.

I'd told it my preferences during a two-hour session last week. We'd worked through my entire note-taking system. By the end, Claude understood exactly how I wanted things structured.

Then the session ended. Everything vanished. [...] • Training preferences my coach established

It took 44 lines of markdown. No database. No API. No vector embeddings.

The system is a Claude Code skill. Skills are instructions that activate on demand. Mine triggers when I say "/remember" or when the AI notices something that seems like a lasting preference.

When triggered, it appends to a simple markdown file. That's it.

The file lives in my repo. Version controlled. I can read it. Edit it. Delete lines that are wrong. Add context the AI missed. It's not a black box. It's a text file.

Setup took five minutes. Copy two files. Done.

Compare this to Mem0's self-hosted version, which requires:

• Python environment setup

• Vector database configuration (Qdrant, Chroma, Pinecone, etc.)

• LLM and embeddings configuration [...] Actionable - Does this change what the AI recommends?

Explicit - Did the user actually say this, or are we inferring?

If a piece of information doesn't pass all three tests, don't save it.

I've been using these exact criteria for months. I didn't call them "distillation criteria." I just called it "not saving garbage."

The OpenAI guide also emphasizes something most memory systems get wrong: forgetting is not a bug. It's essential.

Sophisticated memory tools like Mem0 have solutions for this. Decay mechanisms. Priority scoring. Consolidation algorithms. But these require configuration and tuning. Out of the box, the tendency is to over-store. And when retrieval quality degrades, debugging a vector database is harder than editing a text file.
